title: 'Our infrastructure was designed for a climate that no longer exists': France's unfinished reckoning with increasing heatwaves
subhead: In 2003, an unprecedented heatwave struck France in the middle of August while the government was on vacation. It killed 15,000 people. While this public health shock transformed the alert system and the management of heat-related illnesses, France has failed to learn any lessons regarding its necessary adaptation to climate change.

title: 'I just want to know if he's still alive': The long ordeal of families searching for loved ones lost at sea

'Summer and extreme heat make all the symptoms worse': Coping with a heatwave and mental health conditions
subhead: As temperatures rise, several studies have shown an increase in visits to psychiatric emergency rooms and suicide attempts.

title: Martin Margiela puts 200 pieces from his personal archives up for sale
subhead: On July 9 in Paris, personal items and prototypes from the famously secretive designer will be auctioned. By personally initiating and organizing the sale, Martin Margiela, who left the fashion world in 2008, is offering a glimpse into his creative process. It is a radical gesture that mirrors his artistic journey.

title: 'Is the Gulf Persian or Arabian? Or both?'
subhead: The historical designation of 'Persian Gulf' has faced challenges from Arab regimes, but in 2025, Donald Trump abandoned efforts to replace it with 'Arabian Gulf.'

title: 'The King and the Mockingbird': The animated masterpiece that inspired Studio Ghibli
subhead: His film took more than three decades to reach the screen. In 1946, Paul Grimault, then 41, began work on 'Le Roi et l'Oiseau' ('The King and the Mockingbird') with Jacques Prévert writing the screenplay. He finally saw it screened in 1979, at 74. The hand-drawn animation became a cult classic, inspiring generations of viewers and filmmakers.

250 years of American independence: Frederick Douglass, the former slave turned celebrity who skewered the Republic's hypocrisy
subhead: 'America 250' (6/13). In April 1876, all of Washington's elite gathered to witness the unveiling of a statue of Abraham Lincoln emancipating enslaved people. The main speaker, Frederick Douglass, himself formerly enslaved and one of the most famous faces in the country, was known for directly challenging white Americans. A legendary speech he gave in 1852 proved just that.

title: Heatwave fuels frustration in Paris's northern suburbs: 'If they'd just given us shutters, we wouldn't be suffering like this'
subhead: Over the past week, daily life in two large concrete housing complexes in Seine-Saint-Denis has changed dramatically due to extreme heat. Residents share their struggles and anger as they try to help each other cope.

title: Ukraine's Donbas rear area, from Sloviansk to Kramatorsk, is under constant Russian pressure
subhead: 'Donbas: The War of Attrition' (1/2). Just a few kilometers from one of Ukraine's fiercest fronts, these two cities are under drone strikes that make residents' lives very difficult. Both civilians and soldiers fear the bombings will intensify.
byline: Thomas d’Istria and  Virginie NGuyen Hoang
